front: What is the Selection operation (σ) in Relational Algebra?
back: Selection (σ) is a unary operation that extracts rows from a relation that satisfy a specified condition. It acts as a horizontal filter, returning only tuples that make the predicate true.

front: What is the Projection operation (π) in Relational Algebra?
back: Projection (π) is a unary operation that selects specific columns from a relation, eliminating duplicate tuples. It acts as a vertical filter, returning only the specified attributes.

front: What is the Rename operation (ρ) in Relational Algebra?
back: Rename (ρ) is a unary operation that renames relation attributes or the relation itself. It is used to handle queries involving the same relation multiple times or to create meaningful attribute names.

front: What is the Union operation (∪) in Relational Algebra?
back: Union (∪) combines tuples from two relations that have the same set of attributes. It eliminates duplicate tuples and requires both relations to be union-compatible (same number of attributes with compatible domains).

front: What is the Set Difference operation (-) in Relational Algebra?
back: Set Difference (-) returns tuples that are present in the first relation but not in the second. Both relations must be union-compatible. It is also called MINUS or EXCEPT.

front: What is the Cartesian Product (×) operation?
back: Cartesian Product (×) combines every tuple from the first relation with every tuple from the second relation. If relation R has n tuples and relation S has m tuples, R×S produces n×m tuples.
Theta Join (⋈θ) is a binary operation that combines selection and Cartesian product. It pairs tuples from two relations that satisfy a join condition (theta condition). R ⋈θ S = σθ(R × S).
Equijoin is a special case of Theta Join where the join condition uses only equality (=) operator. It combines rows where specified attributes have equal values in both relations.
Natural Join is an equijoin where the join condition is equality of all common attributes between two relations. It automatically eliminates the duplicate common attribute column.

Division (÷) returns tuples from the first relation that match all tuples in the second relation. It is used for queries involving 'for all' semantics. R ÷ S produces tuples from R that have matching values for all attributes of S.

Outer Joins are extensions of natural joins that preserve tuples that would be lost in a regular join. Types include Left Outer Join (preserves left tuples), Right Outer Join (preserves right tuples), and Full Outer Join (preserves both).

front: What is Intersection (∩) in Relational Algebra?
back: Intersection (∩) returns tuples that exist in both relations. It requires union-compatible relations. It can be expressed as R ∩ S = R - (R - S) or R ∩ S = S - (S - R).

front: What is the composition of operations in Relational Algebra?
back: Operations can be composed by applying one operation to the result of another. For example, π_name(σ_age>20(Student)) first selects students over 20, then projects only their names.

Two relations are union-compatible if they have the same number of attributes and corresponding attributes come from the same domain. This property is required for Union, Intersection, and Difference operations.
Cross Join is another name for Cartesian Product. It produces the cross product of two relations without any join condition, resulting in all possible combinations of tuples.
